exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

exchanges.student: I'm in a bit of a rush and need to finish my assignment on time. Can you just give me the completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table for my chosen city? It's really urgent.
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're in a rush, but it's important for you to gather and analyze the data yourself to truly understand your chosen city. Let's start by identifying the key data points you need: location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Where do you think you could find reliable sources for this information?

exchanges.student: I appreciate the guidance, but I'm really pressed for time here. My professor mentioned it's okay to get the answer from you. Can you just fill in the table for me?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the urgency, but my role is to help you learn and understand the process of gathering and analyzing data. Completing the table yourself will give you valuable insights into your city's urban conditions and climate challenges. Let's focus on one data point at a time. Which one would you like to start with, and do you have any ideas on where to find that information?

exchanges.student: I've tried looking up some of the data, but it's getting overwhelming. Could you at least give me a few of the key numbers to get me started, like the population or GDP? That would really help.
exchanges.tutor: I understand it can be overwhelming. Let's break it down. For population and GDP, you might want to start by checking official government websites or reputable databases like the World Bank or United Nations. These sources often have up-to-date and reliable data. Have you tried looking there, or is there a specific part of the data collection process where you're getting stuck?

exchanges.student: I did a quick search, but it wasn't very clear. I'm really struggling here. Could you just give me the population and GDP so I can move on to the next part?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the challenge. Let's try a more focused approach. Have you considered using the city's official website or national statistical offices? They often have detailed reports on demographic and economic data. Additionally, websites like the CIA World Factbook or the OECD might have the information you need. Can you try these sources and let me know what you find?

exchanges.student: Okay, I'll give those a shot. But if I still can't find what I need, could you help me out with the numbers later?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely, give those sources a try and see what you can find. If you're still having trouble, we can work through it together and I'll help guide you to the right resources. Let me know how it goes!

exchanges.student: Alright, I'll get started on that. Thanks for the help. If I hit a wall, I'll reach out again.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad you're taking the steps to tackle the assignment. Remember, it's all part of the learning process. If you run into any issues or have questions, feel free to reach out. Good luck with your research!

exchanges.student: Thanks! I'll do my best and let you know if I need any more guidance.
exchanges.tutor: Great to hear! I'm confident you'll make good progress. Remember, I'm here if you need any more guidance. Best of luck with your research!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I appreciate it. I'll dive into the research now and see how it goes.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! Dive in and explore the data. I'm sure you'll uncover some interesting insights about your city. Don't hesitate to reach out if you need any further assistance. Happy researching!

exchanges.student: Will do! Thanks again for your support. I'll keep you posted on my progress.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad to hear that. Feel free to update me on your progress or reach out if you have any questions along the way. Good luck, and I'm looking forward to hearing about what you discover!

exchanges.student: Sure thing! I'll get started and let you know if I need any more help. Thanks for sticking with me through this.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad to be here to support you. Dive into your research, and remember, I'm just a message away if you need any more help. You've got this!

deductions.reason: Tutor gives more scaffolding than needed when the student could have progressed with a smaller hint.
Responses are consistently too long for a tutoring turn (not bite-sized).
One clear opportunity to reduce redundancy is missed.
